children.title: Sol-gel synthesis and photoluminescence characterization of \(\mathrm{La_2Ti_2O_7:Eu^{3+}}\) nanocrystals
children.metadata: CHENG Hua; LU Zhouguang; LIU Ying; YANG Haihua; GU Yingying; LI Wei; TANG Yougen; College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Central South University; Nonferrous Metals Society of China; Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg
children.content: CHENG Hua, LU Zhouguang, LIU Ying, YANG Haihua, GU Yingying, LI Wei, and TANG Yougen<|txt_split|>College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, China<|txt_split|>Received 10 November 2010; received in revised form 7 January 2011; accepted 13 January 2011<|txt_split|>© The Nonferrous Metals Society of China and Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 2011

children.title: Abstract
children.metadata: lanthanum titanate; rare earth metals; photoluminescence; nanophosphors; pechini citric acid; sol-gel
children.content: \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\) doped \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_{1 - x}\mathrm{O}_x\) nanocrystals with pure monoclinic phase and size of about \(100\mathrm{nm}\) were prepared by a citric acid (CA) assisted sol-gel method. Techniques of thermo-gravimetric (TG) and differential calorimetry (DCS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), as well as transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were employed to characterize the as-synthesized nanoparticles. Furthermore, photoluminescence (PL) was used to measure the photocurrent values. The PL spectra were evaluated with the effects of calcination temperature and \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\) doping concentration on the photoluminescence properties.<|txt_split|>Keywords: lanthanum titanate; rare earth metals; photoluminescence; nanophosphors; pechini citric acid; sol-gel

subnode.title: Abstract_1
subnode.metadata: Eu³⁺ doped La₂Ti₁₋ₓOₓ nanocrystals; monoclinic phase; citric acid sol-gel method; 100 nm size; TG and DCS characterization; XRD analysis; TEM imaging; PL spectroscopy; photocurrent measurement; calcination temperature effect; Eu³⁺ doping concentration effect
subnode.content: \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\) doped \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_{1 - x}\mathrm{O}_x\) nanocrystals with pure monoclinic phase and size of about \(100\mathrm{nm}\) were prepared by a citric acid (CA) assisted sol-gel method. Techniques of thermo-gravimetric (TG) and differential calorimetry (DCS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), as well as transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were employed to characterize the as-synthesized nanoparticles. Furthermore, photoluminescence (PL) was used to measure the photocurrent values. The PL spectra were evaluated with the effects of calcination temperature and \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\) doping concentration on the photoluminescence properties.

subnode.title: Abstract_2
subnode.metadata: lanthanum titanate; rare earth metals; photoluminescence; nanophosphors; pechini citric acid; sol-gel
subnode.content: Keywords: lanthanum titanate; rare earth metals; photoluminescence; nanophosphors; pechini citric acid; sol-gel

children.title: 1. Introduction
children.metadata: monoclinic crystal structure; layer-perovskite structure; photocatalytic activity; high Curie temperature; dielectric properties; piezoelectric properties; ferroelectric properties; nuclear waste disposal; luminescent nanocrystals; sol-gel synthesis; citric acid chelation; dip coating; spin coating; optical phosphor; UV excitation; 4f5d band; Eu³⁺ doping; nanoscale particle size; compositional homogeneity; ultrafine particles; aqueous sol-gel process
children.content: \(\mathrm{A_2B_2O_3}\) \((\mathrm{A} =\) rare earth metal, \(\mathrm{B} = \mathrm{Ti})\) crystallizes in monoclinic phase with the space group of \(P2_{1}\). The crystal structure of \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_2\mathrm{O}_5\) consists of layers of distorted perovskite-like slab running parallel to the [110] plane, bounded to each other by interlayer \(\mathrm{La^{2 + }}\) ions. The layers contain two different sites for \(\mathrm{La^{2 + }}\) ions in 9-fold coordination, both of which possessing \(C_1\) point symmetry [1-3]. \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_2\mathrm{O}_5\) exhibits high photocatalytic activity for water splitting and decomposition of toxic compounds, such as \(\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{Cl}\) and methyl orange [4-7]. \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_2\mathrm{O}_5\) has found diverse applications in the electronic devices because it has the highest Curie temperature \((T_{c} = 1500^{\circ}\mathrm{C})\) so far, and interesting dielectric, piezoelectric, and ferroelectric properties [8]. \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_2\mathrm{O}_5\) can be used for nuclear waste disposal [9-10]. The robust layer-perovskite structure of \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_2\mathrm{O}_5\) shows excellent performance in terms of high vacuum, as well as high-energy electrons bonding. Both A sites and B sites in \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_2\mathrm{O}_5\) can be substituted by diverse component ions to form stable solid solutions. Furthermore, in the titanate-based compounds, the luminescence of active centers \((\mathrm{Pt^{4 + },Eu^{3 + }}\) etc.) can be excited efficiently in a wide UV range through the 4f5d band [1]. Therefore, \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_2\mathrm{O}_5\) is of special interest as host material for optical active centers to produce phosphors emitting a variety of colors for the development of display devices [1, 8, 11-13].<|txt_split|>Nanoscale particle size is favorable in phosphors due to the merit of processing nanoparticles into devices with high resolution, high screen coverage, intense emission, as well as long service life [14]. The conventional synthesis method for \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_3\mathrm{O}_4\) is solid-state reaction. This method generally causes extensive agglomeration of the particles and compositional inhomogeneity. Therefore, several techniques were developed to synthesize ultrafine \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_3\mathrm{O}_4\) at moderate temperatures, such as co-precipitation [15], liquid mix techniques [16], and sol-gel [17] hydrothermal [6, 18], and sol-gel [19]. Sol-gel processes have attracted much attention because the intimate mixing of components in the sol-gel route ensures composition homogeneity of the final product [19]. Moreover, doping can also be easily achieved through the sol-gel protocols [20].<|txt_split|>Here, we describe an all-aqueous sol-gel approach for the synthesis of \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\) doped \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_2\mathrm{O}_5\) luminescent nanocrystals. The use of citric acid (CA) as chelating agent ensures the formation of homogeneous transparent metal-citrate gels. Most importantly, the sol-gel process offers a unique, easy, stable, and low-cost sol precursor for the future fabrication of high-quality luminescent thin films by dip coating or spin coating.

subnode.title: 1. Introduction_1
subnode.metadata: monoclinic crystal structure; layer-perovskite slab; La²⁺ coordination sites; photocatalytic activity; high Curie temperature; dielectric properties; nuclear waste disposal; solid solution substitution; luminescent active centers; phosphor host material
subnode.content: \(\mathrm{A_2B_2O_3}\) \((\mathrm{A} =\) rare earth metal, \(\mathrm{B} = \mathrm{Ti})\) crystallizes in monoclinic phase with the space group of \(P2_{1}\). The crystal structure of \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_2\mathrm{O}_5\) consists of layers of distorted perovskite-like slab running parallel to the [110] plane, bounded to each other by interlayer \(\mathrm{La^{2 + }}\) ions. The layers contain two different sites for \(\mathrm{La^{2 + }}\) ions in 9-fold coordination, both of which possessing \(C_1\) point symmetry [1-3]. \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_2\mathrm{O}_5\) exhibits high photocatalytic activity for water splitting and decomposition of toxic compounds, such as \(\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{Cl}\) and methyl orange [4-7]. \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_2\mathrm{O}_5\) has found diverse applications in the electronic devices because it has the highest Curie temperature \((T_{c} = 1500^{\circ}\mathrm{C})\) so far, and interesting dielectric, piezoelectric, and ferroelectric properties [8]. \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_2\mathrm{O}_5\) can be used for nuclear waste disposal [9-10]. The robust layer-perovskite structure of \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_2\mathrm{O}_5\) shows excellent performance in terms of high vacuum, as well as high-energy electrons bonding. Both A sites and B sites in \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_2\mathrm{O}_5\) can be substituted by diverse component ions to form stable solid solutions. Furthermore, in the titanate-based compounds, the luminescence of active centers \((\mathrm{Pt^{4 + },Eu^{3 + }}\) etc.) can be excited efficiently in a wide UV range through the 4f5d band [1]. Therefore, \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_2\mathrm{O}_5\) is of special interest as host material for optical active centers to produce phosphors emitting a variety of colors for the development of display devices [1, 8, 11-13].

subnode.title: 1. Introduction_2
subnode.metadata: nanoscale particle size; phosphor performance; solid-state synthesis; ultrafine La₂Ti₃O₄; co-precipitation; sol-gel synthesis; hydrothermal synthesis; composition homogeneity; doping capability
subnode.content: Nanoscale particle size is favorable in phosphors due to the merit of processing nanoparticles into devices with high resolution, high screen coverage, intense emission, as well as long service life [14]. The conventional synthesis method for \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_3\mathrm{O}_4\) is solid-state reaction. This method generally causes extensive agglomeration of the particles and compositional inhomogeneity. Therefore, several techniques were developed to synthesize ultrafine \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_3\mathrm{O}_4\) at moderate temperatures, such as co-precipitation [15], liquid mix techniques [16], and sol-gel [17] hydrothermal [6, 18], and sol-gel [19]. Sol-gel processes have attracted much attention because the intimate mixing of components in the sol-gel route ensures composition homogeneity of the final product [19]. Moreover, doping can also be easily achieved through the sol-gel protocols [20].

subnode.title: 1. Introduction_3
subnode.metadata: all-aqueous sol-gel synthesis; Eu³⁺ doped La₂Ti₂O₅ nanocrystals; citric acid chelation; homogeneous transparent gels; low-cost luminescent thin films
subnode.content: Here, we describe an all-aqueous sol-gel approach for the synthesis of \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\) doped \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_2\mathrm{O}_5\) luminescent nanocrystals. The use of citric acid (CA) as chelating agent ensures the formation of homogeneous transparent metal-citrate gels. Most importantly, the sol-gel process offers a unique, easy, stable, and low-cost sol precursor for the future fabrication of high-quality luminescent thin films by dip coating or spin coating.

children.title: 2. Experimental
children.metadata: tetra-n-butyl titanate; citric acid; La(NO₃)₂; sol-gel synthesis; thermal decomposition; XRD analysis; TEM characterization; photoluminescence; Eu³⁺ doping; annealing process
children.content: All the chemicals used in the experiments were of ana<|txt_contd|>lactical purity, bought from Sigma-Aldrich and used without further purification. In a typical procedure, \(0.01\mathrm{mol}\) tetra-\(n\)-butyl titanate was dissolved in \(50~\mathrm{mL}\) anhydrous ethanol, and \(1\mathrm{mL}\) concentrated nitric acid was added to restrain hydrolyzation of the tetra-\(n\)-butyl titanate. \(0.02\mathrm{mol}\) citric acid was dissolved in \(100~\mathrm{mL}\) deionized water. Then \(0.2\mathrm{mol} / \mathrm{L}\) \(\mathrm{La(NO_3)_2}\) aqueous solution of \(50~\mathrm{mL}\) was added into the CA solution dropwise under vigorous stirring at \(80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) to produce a clear La-citrate mixed solution. After that the La-citrate solution was added into the tetra-\(n\)-butyl titanate ethanol solution dropwise with vigorous stirring and a transparent mixed sol was consequently produced. During this mixing procedure, the temperature was controlled at around \(80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) by using a water bath. The as-obtained sol was kept in a glass flask containing a mixture solvent (water or ethanol) until a transparent and viscous gel was obtained. The as-obtained gel was subsequently transferred to an oven and kept at a temperature of \(160^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) for \(2\mathrm{h}\) to form a loose and black precursor. The precursor was then annealed at a certain temperature for \(3\mathrm{h}\) with a heating rate of \(10^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}\). \(\mathrm{Eu^{3+}}\) doped \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Tf}_3\mathrm{O}_4\) nanocrystals were synthesized by the same route by substituting \(\mathrm{La^{3+}}\) with a desired amount of \(\mathrm{Eu^{3+}}\) in the raw materials preparation.<|txt_split|>The thermal decomposition behavior of the citrate gel precursors was examined by a thermo-gravimetric analyzer (TG/DSC, Perkin Elmer, TAC 7/DX) employing air as working gas. Measurement was conducted with a heating rate of \(10^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}\) from 25 to \(1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\). The crystal phase of the precipitate was determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8) using \(\mathrm{CuK}_{\alpha}\) radiation (\(\lambda = 0.15418\mathrm{nm}\)) at a scan rate of \(4^{\circ}\mathrm{min}\). The morphology of the final products was characterized with a Hitachi H-800 transmission electron microscope (TEM) operated at \(200\mathrm{kV}\). The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained on a JEOL-3010 equipment. The photoluminescence measurements were carried out using a Hitachi F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The excitation wavelengths were corrected for the beam intensity in the Xe laser. For comparison, all excitation and emission spectra were measured at room temperature with constant instrumental parameters.

subnode.title: 2. Experimental_1
subnode.metadata: tetra-n-butyl titanate; citric acid; La(NO₃)₂; sol-gel synthesis; europium doping; annealing; precursor formation; water-ethanol mixture; temperature control; nanocrystal synthesis
subnode.content: All the chemicals used in the experiments were of analactical purity, bought from Sigma-Aldrich and used without further purification. In a typical procedure, \(0.01\mathrm{mol}\) tetra-\(n\)-butyl titanate was dissolved in \(50~\mathrm{mL}\) anhydrous ethanol, and \(1\mathrm{mL}\) concentrated nitric acid was added to restrain hydrolyzation of the tetra-\(n\)-butyl titanate. \(0.02\mathrm{mol}\) citric acid was dissolved in \(100~\mathrm{mL}\) deionized water. Then \(0.2\mathrm{mol} / \mathrm{L}\) \(\mathrm{La(NO_3)_2}\) aqueous solution of \(50~\mathrm{mL}\) was added into the CA solution dropwise under vigorous stirring at \(80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) to produce a clear La-citrate mixed solution. After that the La-citrate solution was added into the tetra-\(n\)-butyl titanate ethanol solution dropwise with vigorous stirring and a transparent mixed sol was consequently produced. During this mixing procedure, the temperature was controlled at around \(80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) by using a water bath. The as-obtained sol was kept in a glass flask containing a mixture solvent (water or ethanol) until a transparent and viscous gel was obtained. The as-obtained gel was subsequently transferred to an oven and kept at a temperature of \(160^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) for \(2\mathrm{h}\) to form a loose and black precursor. The precursor was then annealed at a certain temperature for \(3\mathrm{h}\) with a heating rate of \(10^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}\). \(\mathrm{Eu^{3+}}\) doped \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Tf}_3\mathrm{O}_4\) nanocrystals were synthesized by the same route by substituting \(\mathrm{La^{3+}}\) with a desired amount of \(\mathrm{Eu^{3+}}\) in the raw materials preparation.

subnode.title: 2. Experimental_2
subnode.metadata: thermal decomposition; X-ray diffraction; transmission electron microscopy; photoluminescence measurement; excitation emission spectra
subnode.content: The thermal decomposition behavior of the citrate gel precursors was examined by a thermo-gravimetric analyzer (TG/DSC, Perkin Elmer, TAC 7/DX) employing air as working gas. Measurement was conducted with a heating rate of \(10^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}\) from 25 to \(1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\). The crystal phase of the precipitate was determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8) using \(\mathrm{CuK}_{\alpha}\) radiation (\(\lambda = 0.15418\mathrm{nm}\)) at a scan rate of \(4^{\circ}\mathrm{min}\). The morphology of the final products was characterized with a Hitachi H-800 transmission electron microscope (TEM) operated at \(200\mathrm{kV}\). The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained on a JEOL-3010 equipment. The photoluminescence measurements were carried out using a Hitachi F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The excitation wavelengths were corrected for the beam intensity in the Xe laser. For comparison, all excitation and emission spectra were measured at room temperature with constant instrumental parameters.

children.title: 3.1. Thermal analysis
children.metadata: thermal decomposition; TG-DSC analysis; weight-loss regions; pyrolysis of nitrate; organic phase elimination; La₂Ti₂O₅ crystallization; endothermic peak; exothermic peaks; adsorbed water removal; phase transformation
children.content: The thermal decomposition behavior of the obtained La-Ti-citrate gel precursors was investigated by the TG-DSC method and the results are shown in Fig. 1. The TG curve demonstrates three continuous weight-loss regions in the range of \(25 - 250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) (about \(10\%\)), \(250 - 400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) (about \(35\%\)), and \(400 - 750^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) (about \(12\%\)), respectively. The first<|txt_contd|>weight-loss corresponds to the elimination of physical adsorbed water. The second weight-loss region can be ascribed to the pyrolysis of \(\mathrm{NO}_3^-\) and some organic phases (citric acid). The third weight loss is associated to the elimination of the organic phase, which is dominated by the presence of compounds) and the formation of crystalline \(\mathrm{La_2Ti_2O_5}\) inorganic phase [5]. The DSC line shows four features. The endothermic process around \(100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) is believed to be from the removal of the adsorbed waters. Two major exothermic peaks in the region of \(350 - 450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) are attributed to the decomposition of the organic phase. The exothermic peak occurs at \(100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) and the second weight loss occurs at residual gasics as well as the crystallization of \(\mathrm{La_2Ti_2O_5}\) inorganic phase, which is consistent with the TG analysis.

children.title: Fig.1. TG and DSC curves of the metal-citrate gel precursors.
children.metadata: Thermal analysis; TG; DSC; metal-citrate gel precursors; temperature; weight loss; heat flow
children.content: The image presents a combined thermogravimetric (TG) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) curve for metal-citrate gel precursors. The left y-axis (TG/%) shows weight loss percentage, while the right y-axis (Heat Flow/mW) indicates heat flow in milliwatts. The x-axis represents temperature in degrees Celsius. The TG curve (lower line) shows a gradual decrease in weight, with a steep drop around 400°C, followed by a plateau and then a slight increase. The DSC curve (upper line) shows a peak around 400°C, indicating an exothermic event, followed by a broad endothermic peak around 600°C, and then a negative heat flow region. The arrows labeled "TG" and "DSC" indicate the respective measurement modes. The figure is labeled "Fig. 1." at the bottom.

children.title: 3.2.XRD
children.metadata: XRD patterns; calcination temperature; fine grain size; poor crystallinity; monoclinic La₃Ti₄O₇; layered perovskite structure; phase purity; particle size increase; crystallinity improvement
children.content: Fig. 2 shows the XRD patterns of the as-obtained \((\mathrm{La}_{0.85}\mathrm{Eu}_{0.15})_3\mathrm{Ti}_2\mathrm{O}_7\) nanoparticles as a function of calcination temperature. The broad and weak diffraction peaks of the sample calcined at \(600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) are indicative of a very fine grain size and poor crystallinity. A set of intensive diffractions can be observed in the XRD spectrum, which indicates that all diffraction lines match well with the characteristic reflections of \(\mathrm{La}_3\mathrm{T}_4\mathrm{O}_7\) according to the JCPDS 28-0517 spectrum. The diffraction pattern can be indexed to the monoclinic \(\mathrm{La}_3\mathrm{T}_4\mathrm{O}_7\) with layered perovskite structure [1] and no secondary phases can be observed. When the calcination temperature was further raised to \(1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\), the integral peak intensity becomes much stronger and the full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) becomes narrower, but no secondary peaks are observed in the XRD patterns, revealing that the particle size increases and the crystallinity improves with temperature increasing while remaining phase pure of monoclinic \(\mathrm{La}_3\mathrm{T}_4\mathrm{O}_7\).

children.title: Fig. 2. XRD patterns of the products after the calcining of the metal-biuret gel precursors at (a) \(600\), (b) \(800\), and (c) \(1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) for \(3\mathrm{h}\) in air at \(150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\). The peaks were fixed at \((\lambda_{\mathrm{max}} = \lambda_{\mathrm{emax}} / 3)\mathrm{T}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{5}\), and the bottom bars indicate the observed diffraction lines from \(\mathrm{Li_2Ti_2O_5}\) according to JCPDC-280517.
children.metadata: XRD, calcined, gel precursors, lithium niobate, lithium tantalate, crystalline structure
children.content: The image displays three X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns labeled (a), (b), and (c), corresponding to samples calcined at 600°C, 800°C, and 1000°C for 3 hours in air, respectively. The top graph shows the XRD pattern of the product after calcining the metal-citrate gel precursors, while the bottom graph indicates the observed diffractions from Li₂O–Ti₂O₅ according to JCPDS-280517. Each pattern is marked with diffraction peaks labeled with their 2θ values in degrees, illustrating the crystalline structure evolution with increasing calcination temperature.

children.title: 3.3. TEM and HRTEM
children.metadata: La₀.₅Eu₀.₅Ti₂Ti₂O₃ nanoparticles; uniform nanoparticle morphology; 60 nm grain size; well-defined SAED rings; high crystallinity; HRTEM lattice fringes; Eu³⁺ doping; poly-crystalline structure
children.content: Fig. 3(a) shows the low-magnification TEM image of the<|txt_contd|>as-obtained \((\mathrm{La}_{0.5}\mathrm{Eu}_{0.5}\mathrm{Ti}_2)\mathrm{Ti}_2\mathrm{O}_3\) nanoparticles. It reveals that the sample consists of uniform nanoparticles with a grain size of about \(60~\mathrm{nm}\). The SAED pattern demonstrates well-defined diffraction rings, indicating good crystallinity and poly-crystal nature of the synthesized \((\mathrm{La}_{0.5}\mathrm{Eu}_{0.5}\mathrm{Ti}_2)\mathrm{Ti}_2\mathrm{O}_3\) nanoparticles. The order lattice fringes of the HRTEM images as revealed in Fig. 3(b) further confirms that the \((\mathrm{La}_{0.5}\mathrm{Eu}_{0.5}\mathrm{Ti}_2)\mathrm{Ti}_2\mathrm{O}_3\) nanoparticles are well-crystallized. It also suggests that the \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\) active ions have been effectively doped into the crystal lattice of the \(\mathrm{La}_{1}\mathrm{Ti}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}\) nanoparticles.

children.title: TEM Image of Nanoparticle Aggregates
children.metadata: Transmission electron microscopy; nanoparticles; aggregate; scale bar; diffraction pattern
children.content: The image displays a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) view of a cluster of dark, spherical nanoparticles aggregated together, with a scale bar indicating 100 nm. An inset in the lower-left corner shows the corresponding electron diffraction pattern, which features a central bright spot surrounded by concentric rings, suggesting crystalline structure. The overall image is monochromatic, with varying shades of gray indicating differences in electron density and particle size.

children.title: Fig. 3. (a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of the \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_2\mathrm{O}_7\mathrm{-Eu}^{3+}\) (Eu:Lu = 0.05-0.95) nanoparticles calcined at \(800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) for \(3\mathrm{h}\) (The inset in (a) is the corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern).
children.metadata: TEM, HRTEM, La₂Ti₂O₇:Eu³⁺, nanoparticles, calcination, SAED pattern
children.content: The image displays (a) a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image and (b) a high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of La₂Ti₂O₇:Eu³⁺ nanoparticles calcined at 800°C for 3 hours. The inset in (a) shows the corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern, which reveals the crystalline structure of the nanoparticles. The scale bar in (a) indicates 3 nm, providing a sense of the nanoparticle size.

children.title: 3.4. Photoluminescence
children.metadata: Eu³⁺ luminescence; charge transfer band; red emission; non-inversion symmetry; perovskite-like structure; calcination temperature effect; activator concentration quenching; electric dipole transition; magnetic dipole transition; crystallinity improvement
children.content: Fig.4 shows the room-temperature excitation and emission spectra of the \(\mathrm{(Lu_{0}(Eu_{0}H_{0})_3Ti_2O_7}\) nanophosphorus calcined at \(800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\). The broad band between 220 and \(300\mathrm{nm}\) in the excitation spectrum (Fig.4, left) can be attributed to the<|txt_contd|>charge transfer band (CTB) between the \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\) and the surrounding oxygen anions [21-23]. The emission spectrum (on the right of Fig. 4), mainly appears in the red spectral area (from 550 to \(710\mathrm{~nm}\)), corresponding to transitions from the excited \({}^3\mathrm{D}_8\) state to the \({}^3\mathrm{F}_1\) (\(J = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4\)) levels of the \(4^{\mathrm{th}}\) configuration of \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\) [21]. As shown in Fig. 4, the strongest emission band for the as-obtained \((\mathrm{La}_{0.6}\mathrm{Eu}_{0.48})_{12}\mathrm{Ti}_2\mathrm{O}_5\) nanospheres is centered at around \(614\mathrm{~nm}\) originating from the electric dipole transition \({}^3\mathrm{D}_8 - {}^3\mathrm{F}_2\). While the emission band centered at around \(588\) and \(596\mathrm{~nm}\), originating from the \({}^3\mathrm{D}_8 - {}^3\mathrm{F}_2\) magnetic dipole transition, is much weaker. Theoretically, when the \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\) ions are embedded in a site of non-inversion symmetry, the \({}^3\mathrm{D}_8 - {}^3\mathrm{F}_2\) electric dipole transition is dominant, while in a site with inversion symmetry, the \({}^3\mathrm{D}_8 - {}^3\mathrm{F}_2\) magnetic dipole transition is the strongest [21, 24-25]. As a result, the photoluminescence spectrum reveals<|txt_split|>A non-inversion symmetry environment for the \(\mathrm{Eu^{3+}}\) active centers in the \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_3\mathrm{O}_7\) crystal structure. From a crystallographic point of view, the crystal structure of \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_3\mathrm{O}_7\) consists of distorted perovskite-like slabs running parallel to the [110] plane, bounded to each other by interlayer \(\mathrm{La^{3+}}\) ions [1, 8]. The site symmetry of the \(\mathrm{La^{3+}}\) site is noncentrosymmetric \(\mathrm{C}_2\) in the monoclinic \(P_2\mathrm{l}\) lattice of \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_3\mathrm{O}_7\) at room temperature [8]. Therefore, the \(\mathrm{Eu^{3+}}\) that substitutes the \(\mathrm{La^{3+}}\) should also locate in an asymmetric site, which is consistent with the PL results.<|txt_split|>Fig. 5 summarizes the emission intensity of the \((\mathrm{Lu}_{0.4}\mathrm{Eu}_{0.6}\mathrm{Ti}_{3}\mathrm{O}_{7})\) nanocrystals as a function of the calcination temperature. The emission intensity increases with the calcination temperature from 500 to \(1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\). The weak emission at lower temperature is presumably due to the disorder crystal structure and the remaining quenching centers such as impure \(\mathrm{NO}_3^-\) and \(\mathrm{OH}^-\) ions. When the calcination temperature increases, the crystallinity improves and the<|txt_contd|>quenching centers largely reduce, resulting in a brighter emission.[25-26]<|txt_split|>The effects of the \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\) active center concentration on the emission intensity of the as-synthesized \((\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Eu}_2)\mathrm{Ti}_2\mathrm{O}_7\) nanocrystals are shown in Fig. 6. It reveals that the emission intensity increases with the \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\) concentration, and reaches the strongest emission when the \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\) concentration approaches \(100\%\) [25]. The PL intensity decreases slightly. The PL intensity is highly dependent on the activator \((\mathrm{Eu}^{3+})\) concentration. Generally speaking, the intensity increases with activator concentration up to a certain extent. Above this point, pairing or aggregation of activator atoms leads to efficient resonant energy transfer between \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\) ions and a fraction of energy migration to distant killer or quencher, and quenching behavior appears, resulting in an increase in the PL intensity [27]. It is e-identical that for this type of phosphorus, quenching starts at an \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\) atomic concentration of about \(5\%\).

children.title: Fig. 4. Typical temperature excitation and emission spectra of \(\mathrm{Cu_2O}\) (Eu:La = 0.05/0.95) nanophytes calcined at \(800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) for \(3\mathrm{h}\).
children.metadata: LaF3:Eu3+ phosphor, room temperature, excitation and emission spectra, 800°C calcined, 3 hours
children.content: The image presents a combined excitation and emission spectrum of LaF3:Eu3+ phosphor, calcined at 800°C for 3 hours. The excitation spectrum (left side) shows a peak at 286 nm, with a labeled peak at 644 nm. The emission spectrum (right side) displays multiple peaks: a prominent peak at 286 nm labeled as “D₃/₂–F₁/₂,” a peak at 510 nm labeled as “D₅/₂–F₇/₂,” and another peak at 550 nm labeled as “D₅/₂–F₇/₂.” The x-axis represents wavelength in nm, and the y-axis represents intensity in arbitrary units (a.u.). The figure is labeled as “Fig. 4” and includes a caption describing the phosphor composition (LaF3:Eu3+; Eu:La = 0.05:0.95).

children.title: Fig. 5. The emission intensity \((64\mathrm{nm})\) resulting from the excitation of \(\mathrm{Au}_{0.1}\mathrm{Au}_{0.95}\mathrm{TiO}_2\) and \(\mathrm{Cu}_{0.95}\mathrm{Cu}_{0.85}\mathrm{TiO}_2\) at \(300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) (The composition was fixed at \(1\mathrm{~g}(\mathrm{La}_{0.95}\mathrm{Eu}_{0.85})_{2}\mathrm{TiO}_2\)).
children.metadata: Emission intensity; calcination temperature; Ti₂O₃; 614 nm; (La₀.₆₈Sr₀.₃₂)Ti₂O₇
children.content: The graph displays the emission intensity (in arbitrary units, a.u.) at 614 nm as a function of calcination temperature (in °C), ranging from 500°C to 1000°C. The intensity increases sharply with temperature, rising from approximately 200 a.u. at 500°C to over 1200 a.u. at 1000°C, with data points plotted at intervals of approximately 100°C. The caption indicates that the emission arises from the ³D₁ → ³F₂ transition in Ti³⁺ ions, and the sample composition is fixed as (La₀.₆₈Sr₀.₃₂)Ti₂O₇.

children.title: Spectral Analysis of Sample with Varying Parameters
children.metadata: Spectral plot; intensity vs. wavelength; multiple curves; parameter values 0.01, 0.05, 0.10, 0.15
children.content: The image displays a spectral graph with the y-axis labeled "Intensity / a.u." and the x-axis labeled "Wavelength / nm", ranging from 570 to 650 nm. Four distinct curves are plotted, each corresponding to different parameter values (0.01, 0.05, 0.10, 0.15) as indicated by the legend. The curves show varying peak intensities and positions, with the 0.01 curve exhibiting the highest peak intensity around 620 nm, while the 0.15 curve shows a broader and lower intensity profile. The graph is labeled as (a) in the top left corner.

children.title: Fig. 6. (a) evolution of the emission spectra of the \(\mathrm{La_2TiO_3:Eu^{3+}}\) nanophorons calcified at \(800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) for \(3\mathrm{h}\) with different \(\mathrm{Eu^{3+}}\) doping concentrations, (b) the emission intensity as a function of \(\mathrm{Eu^{3+}}\) concentration, and (c) the emission phenomenon plotted as a function of \(\mathrm{Eu^{3+}}\) active center concentration.
children.metadata: Eu-doped La2Ti2O7:Eu3+ nanophosphors; emission spectra; temperature; doping concentration
children.content: The image displays a graph plotting the intensity (in arbitrary units) of the emission peak at 614 nm (Dy³⁺:F center transition) of La2Ti2O7:Eu³+ nanophosphors versus the Eu doping concentration (ranging from 0 to 0.16). The emission spectra were recorded at 800°C after 3 hours of calcination. The graph shows a peak intensity around 0.08 mol% Eu doping concentration, followed by a gradual decrease in intensity with further increasing Eu concentration. The x-axis is labeled "Eu doping concentration" and the y-axis is labeled "Intensity (a.u.)". A small (b) label indicates a secondary or specific data point in the graph.

subnode.title: 3.4. Photoluminescence_1
subnode.metadata: Eu³⁺ phosphor; charge transfer band; red emission; ³D₈→³F₂ transition; electric dipole; magnetic dipole; non-inversion symmetry; photoluminescence spectrum
subnode.content: Fig.4 shows the room-temperature excitation and emission spectra of the \(\mathrm{(Lu_{0}(Eu_{0}H_{0})_3Ti_2O_7}\) nanophosphorus calcined at \(800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\). The broad band between 220 and \(300\mathrm{nm}\) in the excitation spectrum (Fig.4, left) can be attributed to thecharge transfer band (CTB) between the \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\) and the surrounding oxygen anions [21-23]. The emission spectrum (on the right of Fig. 4), mainly appears in the red spectral area (from 550 to \(710\mathrm{~nm}\)), corresponding to transitions from the excited \({}^3\mathrm{D}_8\) state to the \({}^3\mathrm{F}_1\) (\(J = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4\)) levels of the \(4^{\mathrm{th}}\) configuration of \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\) [21]. As shown in Fig. 4, the strongest emission band for the as-obtained \((\mathrm{La}_{0.6}\mathrm{Eu}_{0.48})_{12}\mathrm{Ti}_2\mathrm{O}_5\) nanospheres is centered at around \(614\mathrm{~nm}\) originating from the electric dipole transition \({}^3\mathrm{D}_8 - {}^3\mathrm{F}_2\). While the emission band centered at around \(588\) and \(596\mathrm{~nm}\), originating from the \({}^3\mathrm{D}_8 - {}^3\mathrm{F}_2\) magnetic dipole transition, is much weaker. Theoretically, when the \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\) ions are embedded in a site of non-inversion symmetry, the \({}^3\mathrm{D}_8 - {}^3\mathrm{F}_2\) electric dipole transition is dominant, while in a site with inversion symmetry, the \({}^3\mathrm{D}_8 - {}^3\mathrm{F}_2\) magnetic dipole transition is the strongest [21, 24-25]. As a result, the photoluminescence spectrum reveals

subnode.title: 3.4. Photoluminescence_2
subnode.metadata: Eu³⁺ active centers; La₂Ti₃O₇ crystal structure; non-inversion symmetry; distorted perovskite-like slabs; noncentrosymmetric C₂ site; interlayer La³⁺ ions; PL results
subnode.content: A non-inversion symmetry environment for the \(\mathrm{Eu^{3+}}\) active centers in the \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_3\mathrm{O}_7\) crystal structure. From a crystallographic point of view, the crystal structure of \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_3\mathrm{O}_7\) consists of distorted perovskite-like slabs running parallel to the [110] plane, bounded to each other by interlayer \(\mathrm{La^{3+}}\) ions [1, 8]. The site symmetry of the \(\mathrm{La^{3+}}\) site is noncentrosymmetric \(\mathrm{C}_2\) in the monoclinic \(P_2\mathrm{l}\) lattice of \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_3\mathrm{O}_7\) at room temperature [8]. Therefore, the \(\mathrm{Eu^{3+}}\) that substitutes the \(\mathrm{La^{3+}}\) should also locate in an asymmetric site, which is consistent with the PL results.

subnode.title: 3.4. Photoluminescence_3
subnode.metadata: calcination temperature; emission intensity; crystallinity; quenching centers; impure ions
subnode.content: Fig. 5 summarizes the emission intensity of the \((\mathrm{Lu}_{0.4}\mathrm{Eu}_{0.6}\mathrm{Ti}_{3}\mathrm{O}_{7})\) nanocrystals as a function of the calcination temperature. The emission intensity increases with the calcination temperature from 500 to \(1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\). The weak emission at lower temperature is presumably due to the disorder crystal structure and the remaining quenching centers such as impure \(\mathrm{NO}_3^-\) and \(\mathrm{OH}^-\) ions. When the calcination temperature increases, the crystallinity improves and the

subnode.title: 3.4. Photoluminescence_4
subnode.metadata: quenching centers; Eu³⁺ concentration; emission intensity; activator aggregation; resonant energy transfer; PL intensity dependence; quenching threshold
subnode.content: quenching centers largely reduce, resulting in a brighter emission.[25-26]The effects of the \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\) active center concentration on the emission intensity of the as-synthesized \((\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Eu}_2)\mathrm{Ti}_2\mathrm{O}_7\) nanocrystals are shown in Fig. 6. It reveals that the emission intensity increases with the \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\) concentration, and reaches the strongest emission when the \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\) concentration approaches \(100\%\) [25]. The PL intensity decreases slightly. The PL intensity is highly dependent on the activator \((\mathrm{Eu}^{3+})\) concentration. Generally speaking, the intensity increases with activator concentration up to a certain extent. Above this point, pairing or aggregation of activator atoms leads to efficient resonant energy transfer between \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\) ions and a fraction of energy migration to distant killer or quencher, and quenching behavior appears, resulting in an increase in the PL intensity [27]. It is e-identical that for this type of phosphorus, quenching starts at an \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\) atomic concentration of about \(5\%\).

children.title: 4.Conclusions
children.metadata: Eu³⁺ doped La₂Ti₂O₇ nanocrystals; citric acid-assisted sol-gel synthesis; La₂TiO₃ perovskite nanoparticles; 60 nm particle size; 800°C annealing; red emission 550–710 nm; 614 nm D₂–F₂ transition; 5 at% Eu³⁺ doping
children.content: In summary, \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\) doped \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{Ti}_2\mathrm{O}_7\) nanocrystals have<|txt_contd|>been successfully synthesized via a facile citric acid-assisted sol-gel approach. Single monolithic phase \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{TiO}_3\) nanoparticles with perovskite structure and the particle size of \(60~\mathrm{nm}\) can be reproducibly obtained at \(800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\). PL characterization shows that after doping \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\) in the \(\mathrm{La}^{3+}\) sites, the as-synthesized \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{TiO}_3\) nanocrystals display dominant emissions in the red area (from 550 to \(710~\mathrm{nm}\)) with the strongest line at \(614~\mathrm{nm}\), corresponding to the \(\mathrm{D}_{2} - \mathrm{F}_{2}\) electric dipole transition of the \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\). The optimized doping concentration of \(\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}\) was found to be around 5 at%.
